title: Sliding Window
description: Maintaining a moving range over an array to avoid recomputing overlapping work -- another O(n) technique replacing an O(n^2) brute force.
objectives: Recognize when a sliding window applies -- contiguous subarrays/substrings
Use a fixed-size window to find a maximum sum
Use a variable-size window for a smallest-subarray-with-a-condition problem

sections.text: 1. The Problem: Recomputing the Same Sums
Suppose we want the maximum sum of any 3 CONSECUTIVE elements in an array. The brute force way checks every group of 3 separately -- and ends up re-adding overlapping elements again and again.
This is O(n * k) -- for every starting position i, the inner loop re-sums all k elements from scratch, even though most of them overlap with the PREVIOUS window. Sliding window eliminates this waste.

A technique that maintains a contiguous range (window) over an array or string, expanding and shrinking it incrementally -- adding one new element and removing one old element per step -- instead of recomputing the entire range from scratch.
3. Fixed-Size Window: Maximum Sum of k Elements

sections.title: Tracing maxSumSlidingWindow({2,1,5,1,3,2}, 3)
steps: i=3: windowSum += arr[3] - arr[0] = 8 + 1 - 2 = 7 -- window is now [1,5,1], maxSum stays 8
i=4: windowSum += arr[4] - arr[1] = 7 + 3 - 1 = 9 -- window is now [5,1,3], maxSum becomes 9
i=5: windowSum += arr[5] - arr[2] = 9 + 2 - 5 = 6 -- window is now [1,3,2], maxSum stays 9

sections.text: windowSum += arr[i] - arr[i - k] is the entire trick: ADD the new element entering the window, SUBTRACT the old element leaving it. This is O(1) work per step, giving O(n) total instead of O(n*k).
4. Variable-Size Window: Smallest Subarray With Sum >= Target

sections.title: How the window grows and shrinks here
points: right expands the window every iteration, adding arr[right] to windowSum
Whenever windowSum >= target, we've found a valid window -- record its length, then SHRINK from the left (subtract arr[left], left++) to look for something even smaller
The answer 2 comes from the subarray [5, 2] (indices 2-3), which sums to 7 -- meeting the target with only 2 elements

sections.text: Unlike the fixed-size window, this window's SIZE changes as we go -- it's called a variable-size (or dynamic) sliding window. Both left and right only ever move forward, never backward, which is exactly why this is still O(n) overall, despite the nested while loop -- similar reasoning to the Two Pointers lesson's total-movement argument.

sections.title: Recognizing a sliding window problem
points: The problem involves a CONTIGUOUS range (subarray or substring), not scattered elements
You're looking for a max/min/count over all such ranges of some size (fixed or variable)
Brute force would recompute overlapping work for every range -- sliding window reuses that work instead

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Recomputing the window's sum from scratch every step instead of adding the new element and subtracting the old one.
Forgetting the fixed-size window's initial sum must be computed separately before the sliding loop begins.
Using a fixed-size window approach for a problem that actually needs a variable-size window (or vice versa) -- check whether the target window SIZE is given or must be discovered.
Forgetting to shrink the window's left edge in a variable-size window, causing it to only grow and never find a truly minimal answer.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is the sliding window technique?
questions.answer: A technique for maintaining a contiguous range (window) over an array or string, incrementally adding one new element and removing one old element per step, instead of recomputing the entire range from scratch for every position.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of a sliding window approach compared to a brute-force nested-loop approach?
questions.answer: Sliding window is typically O(n), since each element is added to and removed from the window at most once. The equivalent brute-force approach is often O(n*k) or O(n^2), recomputing overlapping work repeatedly.

questions.question: What's the difference between a fixed-size and variable-size sliding window?
questions.answer: A fixed-size window always has the same length k, sliding forward by one position at a time. A variable-size window grows and shrinks dynamically based on a condition (like a sum threshold), expanding via a right pointer and shrinking via a left pointer as needed.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: Sliding window maintains a contiguous range over an array or string, updating it incrementally -- adding the newly entering element and removing the one leaving -- instead of recomputing the whole range from scratch, turning an O(n*k) or O(n^2) brute force into O(n). A fixed-size window (like maximum sum of k consecutive elements) slides forward by exactly one position each step. A variable-size window (like the smallest subarray with a sum at least some target) expands via a right pointer and shrinks via a left pointer based on a condition, with both pointers only ever moving forward, keeping the overall approach O(n).

sections.title: Trick to Remember
sections.text: Sliding window = a physical window frame sliding along a wall -- one brick appears on one side, one brick disappears on the other, you never rebuild the whole wall. Fixed size = the frame is a constant width. Variable size = the frame stretches and shrinks as needed.

sections.title: Quick Understanding Check
questions: What problem does sliding window solve compared to brute force?
What's the key update step when sliding a fixed-size window forward by one position?
What's the difference between a fixed-size and variable-size window?
Why is the variable-size window approach still O(n) despite having a nested while loop?

sections.title: Maximum Average Subarray I
sections.problemStatement: Given an integer array nums and an integer k, find the maximum average value of any contiguous subarray of length k, and return it.

examples.explanation: Window [12,-5,-6,50] sums to 51, average 51/4 = 12.75, the best of all length-4 windows.

hints: This IS the fixed-size window max-sum pattern from this lesson's Section 3 -- just divide the max sum by k at the end.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: For every window start, sum all k elements from scratch.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Fixed-size sliding window: compute the first window's sum, then slide by adding the entering element and subtracting the leaving one.

sections.title: Minimum Size Subarray Sum
sections.problemStatement: Given an array of positive integers nums and a positive integer target, return the minimal length of a contiguous subarray whose sum is greater than or equal to target. If there is no such subarray, return 0.

examples.explanation: The subarray [4,3] has the minimal length under the problem constraint.

hints: This is exactly this lesson's variable-size window example, smallestSubarrayLength -- same technique, different variable names.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Check every subarray's sum directly, tracking the shortest one meeting the target.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Variable-size sliding window: expand with right, shrink with left whenever the sum meets target.

sections.title: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters
sections.problemStatement: Given a string s, find the length of the longest substring without repeating characters.
constraints: s consists of English letters, digits, symbols and spaces
hints: This is exactly the Advanced Problem-Solving Patterns capstone's combined Sliding Window + Hashing example -- a Set tracks characters currently in the window.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Check every substring for uniqueness using a Set, tracking the longest valid one.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Variable-size window with a HashSet tracking the window's characters, shrinking from the left whenever a duplicate is found.

sections.title: Permutation in String
sections.problemStatement: Given two strings s1 and s2, return true if s2 contains a permutation of s1 (i.e. some substring of s2 is an anagram of s1).

examples.explanation: s2 contains "ba", a permutation of s1.

constraints: s1 and s2 consist of lowercase English letters
hints: A fixed-size window of length s1.length() slides across s2 -- if the window's character COUNTS ever match s1's character counts exactly, a permutation exists.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: For every window of length s1.length() in s2, sort both and compare -- correct, but wasteful.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n * m log m), where m is s1's length

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Fixed-size sliding window maintaining character COUNT arrays for s1 and the current window, comparing counts directly (no sorting).

sections.title: Find All Anagrams in a String
sections.problemStatement: Given two strings s and p, return an array of all the start indices of p's anagrams in s.

examples.explanation: The substring at index 0 is "cba", an anagram of "abc". The substring at index 6 is "bac", also an anagram.

constraints: s and p consist of lowercase English letters
hints: Same fixed-size sliding window + character-count technique as Permutation in String -- just collect EVERY matching start index instead of stopping at the first.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: For every window of length p.length(), sort both and compare.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n * m log m), where m is p's length

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Fixed-size sliding window with character count arrays, sliding across s and comparing counts in O(26) per step.

sections.title: Longest Repeating Character Replacement
sections.problemStatement: Given a string s and an integer k, you can replace up to k characters in s with any other uppercase English letter. Return the length of the longest substring containing the same letter after performing this replacement.

examples.explanation: Replace the two 'A's or two 'B's to get "AAAA" or "BBBB".

constraints: s consists of only uppercase English letters
hints: A window is valid if (window length) - (count of its MOST FREQUENT character) <= k -- that's how many characters would need replacing.
Track character counts within the window; shrink from the left whenever the window becomes invalid.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: For every window, count character frequencies from scratch and check validity.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Variable-size sliding window tracking character counts and the max frequency seen -- shrink from the left only when the window becomes invalid.

sections.title: Minimum Window Substring
sections.problemStatement: Given two strings s and t, return the minimum window substring of s such that every character in t (including duplicates) is included in the window. If no such substring exists, return the empty string.
constraints: s and t consist of English letters
hints: Track how many of each character in t are still NEEDED inside the current window; expand right until all are satisfied, then shrink left as much as possible while still valid.

This is the most involved variable-size window in this course -- it combines Sliding Window with Hashing (a frequency map) exactly like the capstone lesson's combined example, just with a trickier validity condition.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Check every substring of s and verify it contains all of t's characters -- correct but very slow.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n^3) roughly -- substrings times a linear check each

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Variable-size window with a need-count map: expand right until all of t is covered, then shrink left as much as possible while staying valid, tracking the smallest valid window seen.

sections.title: Sliding Window Maximum
sections.problemStatement: Given an array nums and a sliding window of size k moving from the left to the right, return the maximum value in the window at each position it stops at.
hints: This exact problem is solved step by step in the Deque Deep Dive lesson (Stacks & Queues module), using a monotonic deque of indices.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: For every window position, scan all k elements to find the max.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Monotonic deque of indices, kept in decreasing value order -- identical to the Deque Deep Dive lesson's approach.
